text: The surgeon begins the cholecystectomy by working on the lateral aspect of the gallbladder, maintaining hemostasis while avoiding the cystic duct. The gallbladder is mobilized for easier dissection towards the triangle, revealing a notably short cystic duct. The surgeon dissects along the artery, crossing the peritoneum over it, before proceeding with medial dissection.

The surgeon attempts to dissect the gallbladder from the liver bed, focusing on the medial side to achieve the critical view. The cystic duct is clearly visible, and the surgeon works to clear the gallbladder from above the cystic artery, adjusting retraction for better visibility. Scissors are used to carefully maneuver around the hepatic artery without spreading to avoid bleeding.

The surgeon achieves a critical view of the liver bed through the triangle, focusing on mobilizing the gallbladder above the cystic artery. Despite considering taking the duct and artery, the proximity of the hepatic artery prompts caution. Bleeding occurs, leading to a decision to switch to a top-down approach for further dissection.

The surgeon performs a top-down approach, exposing a posterior branch and clearing the back surface of the artery. The hepatic artery is visible, and the surgeon prepares to take the artery and the duct to complete the procedure. The cystic duct appears short, and the common duct is notably dilated.
